parentQuestion: A patient with severe burns shows minimal scarring after healing, with near-complete regeneration of the epidermis. However, deep dermal burns in the same patient resulted in thick, fibrotic scars. What determines whether repair results in regeneration versus fibrosis?

scaffolds.q: What is regeneration in tissue repair?
options: Restoring the original tissue type with full function
scaffolds.explain: REGENERATION means RESTORING the ORIGINAL TISSUE TYPE with full function. The damaged tissue is replaced with identical, functional tissue — the best outcome!

wrongExplains.0: Scar formation is fibrosis, not regeneration.
wrongExplains.3: Infection prevention is separate from tissue regeneration.

scaffolds.q: What is fibrosis in tissue repair?
options: Replacing damaged tissue with scar tissue
scaffolds.explain: FIBROSIS means replacing damaged tissue with SCAR TISSUE. Scar tissue is collagen-rich but doesn't function like the original tissue.

wrongExplains.0: Perfect restoration is regeneration, not fibrosis.
wrongExplains.2: New blood vessel growth is angiogenesis.
wrongExplains.3: Removing dead cells is part of inflammation.

scaffolds.q: Do all tissues regenerate equally well?
options: No, some tissues regenerate well (skin, bone, liver), others poorly (heart, brain)
scaffolds.explain: TISSUE TYPE MATTERS. Epithelia and bone have stem cells and regenerate well. Cardiac muscle and brain tissue don't regenerate well — they form scars instead.

wrongExplains.0: Regenerative capacity varies greatly by tissue type.
wrongExplains.2: Many tissues can regenerate, not just bone.
wrongExplains.3: Multiple tissue types regenerate, not just skin.

scaffolds.q: Does injury severity affect whether regeneration or fibrosis occurs?
options: Yes, mild damage may regenerate while severe damage often scars
scaffolds.explain: The SEVERITY of damage affects outcome. MILD damage may regenerate. SEVERE damage often leads to scarring because too much structure is lost.

wrongExplains.0: Both tissue type AND severity matter.
wrongExplains.2: Mild damage regenerates better than severe.

scaffolds.q: Why do superficial burns often heal without scarring?
options: The basement membrane remains intact, allowing cell migration
scaffolds.explain: Superficial burns leave the BASEMENT MEMBRANE INTACT. This membrane serves as a scaffold for epithelial cells to migrate and regenerate new skin.

wrongExplains.3: Speed isn't the key factor; scaffold preservation is.

scaffolds.q: Why do deep burns result in scarring?
options: They destroy the basement membrane, eliminating the regeneration scaffold
scaffolds.explain: Deep burns DESTROY THE BASEMENT MEMBRANE. Without this scaffold/template for regeneration, the body defaults to SCAR TISSUE (fibrosis).

wrongExplains.0: Bleeding amount isn't the determining factor.
wrongExplains.2: Infection isn't universal in deep burns.
wrongExplains.3: Deep burns heal slowly and scar.

scaffolds.q: What two factors together determine if a wound regenerates or scars?
options: Tissue type and severity of damage
scaffolds.explain: BOTH TISSUE TYPE and SEVERITY OF DAMAGE determine outcome. Regenerative capacity + injury depth together determine whether you get regeneration or fibrosis.

wrongExplains.0: Age can affect healing but isn't the primary determinant.
wrongExplains.2: Location matters somewhat, but tissue type and severity are key.
wrongExplains.3: Environmental factors aren't the main determinants.

scaffolds.q: Why did the same patient have different outcomes for superficial vs. Deep burns?
options: Different depths of injury — superficial preserved basement membrane, deep didn't
scaffolds.explain: Same patient, DIFFERENT DEPTHS = different outcomes. SUPERFICIAL burn regenerated (basement membrane intact). DEEP burn scarred (basement membrane destroyed).

wrongExplains.0: The immune response was the same; depth differed.
wrongExplains.2: Skin color doesn't determine regeneration vs. Scarring.
wrongExplains.3: The scenario doesn't mention different treatments.

scaffolds.q: What determines whether repair results in regeneration versus fibrosis?
options: Tissue type and severity of damage
Only the amount of granulation tissue
scaffolds.explain: TISSUE TYPE and SEVERITY OF DAMAGE determine outcome. Not just age, not just granulation presence, not just inflammation. Both factors together decide regeneration vs. Fibrosis.

wrongExplains.0: Age is one factor but not the only determinant.
wrongExplains.2: Granulation tissue is part of healing but doesn't determine outcome alone.
wrongExplains.3: Inflammation is necessary but doesn't determine final outcome.
